article_title: Landmark Jury Verdicts Find Meta and Google Liable for Addictive Platform Design, Opening the Door to Thousands of Pending Lawsuits

content_preview.title: Landmark Jury Verdicts Find Meta and Google Liable for Addictive Platform Design, Opening the Door to Thousands of Pending Lawsuits
content_preview.summary: Juries in Los Angeles and New Mexico found Meta and Google liable for addictive platform design and child safety failures, awarding $381 million and setting a legal precedent for thousands of pending cases.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Two jury verdicts delivered in the final week of March 2026 have established the first successful legal theory for holding social media companies liable for the addictive design of their platforms, a development that legal analysts say could fundamentally alter how courts evaluate technology products nationwide.

On March 25, a Los Angeles Superior Court jury found Meta and Google liable for deliberately designing Instagram and YouTube to be addictive, awarding $6 million in damages...

editor_notes.content_quality: Exceptional analysis piece covering two landmark verdicts with legal theory, internal evidence, and implications. Well-structured at 1,222 words.
editor_notes.source_verification: The Conversation: verified — Section 230 bypass, Lanier quote, Project Myst, Nicholls 'big tobacco moment', liability split, damages all confirmed. Scientific American: verified — Bejar testimony, Carnegie Mellon 21-min reduction, Dickinson slot machine quote, 10,000+ cases, 800 school districts confirmed. NPR: timed out. CNBC: 403. Key claims from both are corroborated by The Conversation and Scientific American.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All verifiable legal details, damages, and quotes confirmed.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and analytical. Presents legal theory without advocacy.
editor_notes.originality: Fresh topic — landmark verdicts from March 24-25.
concerns: NPR timed out and CNBC returned 403, but core claims are cross-verified
